assignment.name: lit_review
assignment.title: Write a focused literature review on retrieval-augmented generation
assignment.description: Produce a literature review surveying the development of retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for large language models. Identify major design families, representative papers, and open research questions, with a clear thesis about where the field is heading.
constraints: Cite at least 20 peer-reviewed or arXiv papers, all post-2019.
Must include a taxonomy diagram of RAG architectures.
Must end with a section identifying at least 3 open research questions.
All claims about empirical results must be cited to a specific paper.

recommended_plan: Chronological narrative tracing RAG evolution from early hybrid systems to current state-of-the-art (story-driven approach)
Three major families: retriever architecture (dense vs. sparse), integration points (naive vs. iterative vs. in-context), and ranking/reranking strategies
2D matrix diagram (retriever type × integration point) with representative papers plotted

root.decision: Scope of literature review: breadth vs. depth

verdict.notes: The plan is well-structured and feasible, but the 2D matrix diagram may underrepresent the complexity—consider upgrading to a 3D taxonomy or nested diagram to capture ranking strategies as a full third dimension. The chronological narrative is engaging but risks diluting focus; lead with the taxonomy thesis, then use history to justify it, to maximize clarity within 8 pages.

root.chosen_best_option: Chronological narrative tracing RAG evolution from early hybrid systems to current state-of-the-art (story-driven approach)

branches.option: Comprehensive survey covering RAG from first principles through latest variants (wider net, shallower per-method)

history: Comprehensive survey covering RAG from first principles through latest variants (wider net, shallower per-method)

verdict.notes: The layered organization is well-suited to an 8-page format and should produce a coherent taxonomy. However, the thesis about 'convergence toward end-to-end learned pipelines with minimal components' risks being contradicted by recent literature (e.g., hybrid retrieval, modular reranking stages)—verify this claim against at least 5 recent papers before finalizing, or reframe it as a trend with competing alternatives to avoid credibility issues.

child.decision: Thesis positioning: how to characterize RAG field trajectory

verdict.notes: The plan is solid but risks becoming descriptive rather than analytical. The thesis about 'convergence toward unified frameworks' is promising but underspecified—clarify early what consolidation means empirically (e.g., hybrid methods outperforming pure strategies) to give the review argumentative force. The two-axis taxonomy (retrieval strategy × augmentation timing) is clean and manageable within 8 pages if strictly enforced; consider front-loading your matrix/diagram and using it to structure paper sections, which will help stay focused and meet the citation requirement naturally.

The two-axis taxonomy (retrieval strategy × augmentation timing) is practical, though organizing 20+ papers across 6-8 cells risks shallow treatment of some families—prioritize depth in 3-4 key variants and use the remainder as supporting evidence.

branches.option: RAG as foundational technique shifting focus from retrieval design to generation quality under retrieved context (implies the bottleneck has moved)

verdict.notes: The plan is solid and addresses all constraints, but the thesis about 'bottleneck migration' risks overstatement without careful empirical grounding—ensure you cite specific papers showing generation-over-retrieval tradeoffs. The dual-axis taxonomy (strategy × timing) is sensible but may create 6+ cells; keep the diagram readable within space constraints by consolidating or using hierarchical nesting rather than a matrix.

branches.option: Hierarchical taxonomy with primary split (e.g., retriever-first vs. generator-first) then nested sub-categories by application

history: Comprehensive survey covering RAG from first principles through latest variants (wider net, shallower per-method)

verdict.notes: The plan attempts appropriate scope (comprehensive RAG survey) and includes required elements (taxonomy, open questions, citations). However, organizing primarily by application domain risks obscuring the architectural design families that should anchor a focused literature review—consider inverting to lead with architecture patterns (dense vs. sparse retrieval, in-context vs. fine-tuned, iterative refinement) and use applications as evidence of when each excels. The hierarchical taxonomy is sound but ensure the primary split you choose (retriever-first vs. generator-first) actually explains the most important differences in the field; many papers might fit both categories, weakening analytical clarity.

branches.option: Deep dive into 3-4 major RAG design families with extensive architectural detail (narrower focus, greater depth)

history: Deep dive into 3-4 major RAG design families with extensive architectural detail (narrower focus, greater depth)

verdict.notes: The plan's narrower, deeper focus risks leaving the literature review feeling incomplete—a survey typically needs broader coverage of design families rather than exhaustive depth in 3-4. The strong emphasis on hybrid retrieval and theoretical gaps is strategically sound and differentiates the thesis, but ensure you still map the wider landscape (e.g., at least mention retriever training, generation-augmented retrieval, and multi-hop variants) even if briefly, so readers see the full taxonomy before you zoom in. Allocate ~1.5 days to comprehensive paper collection upfront to avoid citation gaps later.

child.chosen_best_option: Prioritize hybrid retrieval methods (dense+sparse fusion) as the emerging dominant approach across all 3-4 families

branches.option: Focus on dense retrieval (learned embeddings, FAISS-style) as primary design family, treat sparse/hybrid as secondary

history: Deep dive into 3-4 major RAG design families with extensive architectural detail (narrower focus, greater depth)
Focus on dense retrieval (learned embeddings, FAISS-style) as primary design family, treat sparse/hybrid as secondary

verdict.notes: The plan shows strong depth focus but risks underrepresenting the field's breadth—a literature review typically surveys the landscape rather than deep-diving into a subset. The emphasis on dense retrieval may limit your ability to credibly identify "major design families" and open questions, since sparse/hybrid methods are actively competing approaches (BM25+LLM, ColBERT, etc.). Consider rebalancing: allocate 60% to breadth (sampling across families) and 40% to depth (detailed case studies), ensuring your taxonomy diagram and open questions reflect genuine design space rather than a particular subcommunity.

child.chosen_best_option: Focus on representative case studies (2-3 papers) showing trade-offs between design families rather than comprehensive performance survey

branches.option: Synthesize existing benchmarks (MS MARCO, Natural Questions, etc.) with comparative tables of model performance across families

Treating dense/sparse retrieval as co-equal design families will consume ~2 pages with limited novelty, leaving insufficient space for the required taxonomy, open questions, and representative paper coverage across the full spectrum of RAG variants (e.g., iterative retrieval, multi-hop reasoning, generation-augmented retrieval). Refocus on: (1) evolution timeline of RAG approaches, (2) major design dimensions as a matrix rather than separate deep dives, (3) augmentation strategies as one dimension within a unified taxonomy, ensuring the 20-paper requirement reflects actual methodological diversity.

verdict.notes: The chronological + 2-family taxonomy is solid and achievable within page limits, but decision #3 (dense retrieval only) significantly weakens the review's scope and comprehensiveness. RAG literature spans sparse retrieval, hybrid BM25+dense, reranking, and iterative retrieval—omitting these creates a distorted picture of the field. Recommend pivoting to a 3-4 family taxonomy (e.g., retrieval mechanism types + fusion patterns) to capture mainstream diversity while staying focused, and allocating ~1.5 pages to open questions as required.
